lesson.menu_title: The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech People
lesson.menu_desc: Reading a sprint update + relative clauses -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- The Sprint Review -- Technical Updates for Non-Tech People
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 5 -- The Sprint Review

paras: Every two weeks, my team holds a sprint review. A sprint is a short period of work, usually two weeks, in which we build one small part of the product. The sprint review is the meeting where we show that work to the people who pay for the project and use it. Most of them are not engineers, so my real job in the room is to explain our progress to an outsider, in plain terms.

I never open with jargon; if a word is too technical, I rephrase it in plain English. Instead of saying that we cleared the backlog, I say we finished the list of tasks that were waiting. Instead of a new feature, I talk about a new button that lets a customer reset a password alone. I also like a simple analogy: our app is like a house, and this sprint we built the front door. A good summary gives the big picture first, and the small details only if someone asks for them.

Of course, not everything goes to plan. This sprint we found a bug that slowed the login screen, so one feature is not ready yet. I tell the stakeholders the truth, but I keep it simple: the part that handles photos needs one more week. Then I show the demo, the short live version of what already works, because people trust what they can see. A review that anyone can follow is a review that builds confidence.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from an internal agile sprint review summary

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this text?
choices: The team has cancelled the project because of a serious bug.
A project manager explains how to present a sprint's technical work to non-technical people in clear, simple language.
The text is a list of every line of code the team wrote this sprint.

items: A sprint is a short period, usually two weeks, when the team builds one part of the product.
The text defines a sprint as a short period, usually two weeks, in which the team builds one small part of the product.
Most people in the sprint review are engineers.
The text says most of them are outsiders, not engineers, so the manager must explain in plain terms.
The manager prefers to open the review with technical jargon.
He says he never opens with jargon; he rephrases technical words and uses an analogy.
One feature is not ready because a bug slowed the login screen.
The text says they found a bug that slowed the login screen, so one feature is not ready yet.
In your own updates, how often do you replace jargon with plain words? Could you do it more?
The manager uses an analogy (the app is like a house). What analogy could you use for a project you are working on now?
He shows a demo because people trust what they can see. When is a live demo better than a slide, and when is it riskier?

listenings.text: Hi team, this is Rafael with a quick voice note before tomorrow's sprint review. Remember who is in the room: most of our guests are not engineers, so we explain everything in plain terms, no jargon. Here is the plan. I will open with a one-line overview: this sprint we made the login faster and we added a new button that lets customers reset their own password. Then I will use the house analogy, because people remember it. Our app is like a house, and this sprint we built the front door. After that, the honest part. We found a bug that slowed the login screen, so the photo feature is not ready yet. We do not hide it; we simply say the part that handles photos needs one more week. Please keep your section short and avoid words like backlog, endpoint, or refactor. If you must use a technical word, define it in one simple sentence. And finally, we end with the demo, the short live version of what already works, because people trust what they can see, not what they only hear. Thanks, everyone, and let's keep it clear and calm.

Hi Rafael, this is Sofia from marketing, leaving you a short voicemail after the sprint review. First of all, thank you. That was the clearest update I have seen from your team. The house analogy really helped, and I finally understand what the front door means for our customers. A couple of small things for next time. When you mentioned the backlog, I was a little lost for a second, so a one-line definition, like the list of tasks that are still waiting, would help people like me. I also loved the demo. Seeing the password button work live was worth more than ten slides, so please keep doing that. On the bug with the photo feature, I appreciate the honesty. You said one more week, which is fine, but it would help if you tell us what the customer will and will not be able to do until then. Marketing has to manage what we promise. Send me one plain-English line I can share with the wider team, and we are good. Great job today, Rafael, really.
